title: Why distributed time is hard
description: Independent clocks drift and NTP only bounds skew loosely, so wall-clock ordering across nodes is unsafe; logical clocks capture causality instead.
body: Use when a design wants to order or expire events using time. Physical clocks on different nodes drift and NTP corrections can jump backward, so two events' wall-clock timestamps cannot reliably establish which happened first; bounded-uncertainty clocks (TrueTime) work only with explicit error intervals and waits. Lamport clocks give a consistent total order proxy and vector clocks detect true concurrency. Do not use timestamps as version numbers across nodes, and avoid expiry logic that assumes synchronized clocks. Actions: replace cross-node time comparisons with logical clocks or uncertainty-aware waits. Validation signals include correct causality classification under injected skew. Risk boundary: last-write-wins by wall clock silently drops writes under skew; treat it as data-loss-prone and prefer causal merge.
